county_metadata.record_losses: Some early records were damaged or lost during the British burning of Washington on August 24, 1814, though most land and court records survived. Additional losses occurred in scattered courthouse fires and administrative relocations throughout the 19th century, but DC's record survival is generally good from the 1790s forward.
county_metadata.jurisdictional_notes: The District of Columbia was established by the Residence Act of 1790 and formally organized in 1791. The original 100-square-mile diamond was carved from Maryland (the eastern portion, including Georgetown) and Virginia (the western portion, including Alexandria). The Virginia portion was retroceded in 1846, so modern DC consists only of the Maryland-ceded territory. Georgetown (incorporated 1789, predates DC) maintained separate municipal government until 1871. Washington City was incorporated in 1802. The three entities (Georgetown, Washington City, and Washington County -- the rural area outside both cities) were consolidated into a single territorial government in 1871 and then placed under appointed commissioners in 1874. DC had no elected local government from 1874 until Home Rule in 1973. For genealogical purposes, DC functions as a single jurisdiction with all local records centralized. Slavery was legal in DC until 1862 -- the DC Compensated Emancipation Act freed enslaved persons nine months before the Emancipation Proclamation. DC's large free Black community and post-Civil War freedmen migration make African American records particularly rich.
county_metadata.research_tips: DC genealogical research requires distinguishing between NATIONAL repositories located in Washington (which serve the whole country) and DC-SPECIFIC records. The National Archives (NARA) on Pennsylvania Avenue and Archives II in College Park, MD, hold federal records -- for DC-local records, focus on: (1) DC Superior Court (formerly DC Supreme Court) for probate, civil cases, and equity records from the 1800s; (2) DC Recorder of Deeds for land records from 1792, including grantor/grantee indexes -- many early deed books are digitized on FamilySearch; (3) DC Archives for local government records, board of health records, and almshouse records; (4) Historical Society of Washington, DC for manuscripts, photographs, city directories (from 1822), and neighborhood histories. For vital records: DC birth and death certificates from 1874 are at DC Vital Records Division; marriages from 1811 are at DC Superior Court (marriage license bureau). Church records are exceptionally rich -- Christ Church Georgetown (Episcopal, 1794), St. Patrick's Catholic Church (1794), Metropolitan AME Church (1838), and dozens of congregations have baptism, marriage, and burial records predating civil registration. For African American research: Howard University's Moorland-Spingarn Research Center is the premier national repository; Georgetown University's Slavery Archive documents the 1838 sale of 272 enslaved persons by Maryland Jesuits; DC's Compensated Emancipation Act (1862) petitions name slaveholders and enslaved persons -- available at NARA and online. The Freedmen's Bureau records at NARA are critical for post-Civil War DC families. Tax assessment records from the 1800s at DC Archives list property owners by ward. FamilySearch has extensive DC microfilm including deed books, probate records, and church records.
county_metadata.online_land_records_scope: FamilySearch has digitized DC deed books from 1792; DC Recorder of Deeds maintains current records online; historical grantor/grantee indexes available

source_profiles.source_name: DC Land Records -- Recorder of Deeds / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Deed books from 1792 covering all real property transactions in the District; grantor/grantee indexes; early books include Georgetown and Washington County transactions; FamilySearch has digitized many volumes; DC Recorder of Deeds has modern online search
source_profiles.survival_notes: Substantially complete from 1792; some early volumes show wear but are legible; the British burning of Washington in 1814 damaged some government records but most land records survived

source_profiles.source_name: DC Probate Records -- DC Superior Court / NARA
source_profiles.repository: DC Superior Court, Probate Division / NARA
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Wills, administrations, inventories, and estate records from 1801; DC Circuit Court (later Supreme Court, now Superior Court) handled probate; earlier records (1791-1801) may be found in Maryland county courts; orphans court records document guardianships and minor estates
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good survival from 1801; some gaps in early 19th century records due to administrative transitions between Maryland and DC courts

source_profiles.source_name: DC Marriage Records -- Superior Court / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Marriage licenses and returns from 1811; licenses issued by the Clerk of the Circuit Court (later Superior Court); returns from officiating clergy provide additional detail; FamilySearch has indexed DC marriages; earlier marriages found in Maryland records or church registers
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1811 with minor gaps; pre-1811 marriages must be reconstructed from church records and Maryland county records

source_profiles.source_name: DC Death Records -- Vital Records Division / DC Archives
source_profiles.repository: DC Department of Health, Vital Records Division / DC Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Death certificates from 1874; DC Board of Health began registration; earlier deaths documented in church burial records, newspaper obituaries, and cemetery records; Board of Health burial permits from 1855 at DC Archives provide a partial bridge
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1874; earlier Board of Health records (1855-1874) are fragmentary but useful

source_profiles.source_name: DC Federal Census Records -- NARA / FamilySearch / Ancestry
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Federal census from 1800 (DC first enumerated separately); 1790 census lists DC residents under Maryland; slave schedules (1850, 1860) list slaveholders and number of enslaved persons; 1867 DC voter registration (special enumeration); 1878 DC police census at DC Archives
source_profiles.survival_notes: Complete from 1800; the 1890 census was largely destroyed by fire nationally; DC's 1867 and 1878 special enumerations partially compensate

source_profiles.source_name: DC Church Records -- Multiple Denominations / FamilySearch
source_profiles.repository: Various congregations / Historical Society of Washington, DC / FamilySearch
source_profiles.coverage_notes: Exceptionally rich church records from DC's founding: Christ Church Georgetown (Episcopal, 1794), St. Patrick's Catholic (1794), Metropolitan AME (1838), Foundry Methodist (1814), First Baptist (1802), and many others. Baptisms, marriages, burials, and communicant lists. FamilySearch has microfilmed many DC church records. The Catholic Archdiocese of Washington maintains sacramental registers. African American congregations (AME, Baptist) have critical records for the free Black community and post-Civil War freedmen.
source_profiles.survival_notes: Good to excellent; many congregations have continuous records from their founding; some records deposited at Historical Society of Washington, DC or at denominational archives

source_profiles.source_name: DC Emancipation and Slavery Records -- NARA / Georgetown University
source_profiles.repository: NARA / Georgetown University Slavery Archive / DC Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: DC Compensated Emancipation Act petitions (1862) name slaveholders and enslaved persons with physical descriptions -- digitized and searchable online; Georgetown University Slavery Archive documents the 1838 sale of 272 enslaved persons by Maryland Jesuits; DC slave trade records; manumission papers filed with DC courts; Freedmen's Bureau records (1865-1872) at NARA document the transition from slavery to freedom
source_profiles.survival_notes: Emancipation petitions are complete and well-preserved; pre-1862 slavery records are scattered across deed books (slave sales), court records, and estate inventories; Georgetown archive is actively expanding

source_profiles.source_name: DC Military and Civil War Records -- NARA / DC Archives
source_profiles.coverage_notes: DC militia rolls from 1802; Civil War defenses of Washington (ring of forts including Fort Stevens, Fort DeRussy, Fort Totten) employed DC residents; USCT (United States Colored Troops) records for DC-recruited regiments at NARA; DC draft registrations (WWI, WWII); veterans burial records at Congressional Cemetery, Arlington National Cemetery (Virginia), and Soldiers' Home National Cemetery
source_profiles.survival_notes: Federal military records at NARA are extensive; DC militia records are fragmentary for early period but improve after 1840; Civil War records are comprehensive
